name: Circular gravel from Quesa to Caroche Peak
name_original: Circular gravel desde Quesa al pico Caroche
description:
The interior of the province of Valencia is full of mountains. The Sierra de los Caroches is located almost in Castilla-La Mancha, in the heart of the Canal de Navarrés region, its steep limestone massifs are the ideal place for the water to play with the soft terrain and carve spectacular gorges of turquoise pools.

This Route is circular from Quesa, an important rural town surrounded by ideal swimming areas in summer that you can reach by private vehicle. The path ascends to the Caroche peak, while immersing you in a charming Mediterranean mountain landscape dotted with plots of olive trees, almond trees and some fruit trees, crowned by imposing calcareous cliffs.

The route is physically demanding and during the day you do not pass through any town, so we recommend that you bring enough water and food and some spare parts. The technical section is not difficult, so a gravel bike is more than enough to ride the slopes and enjoy the views. Given the high temperature contrast between winter and summer, we advise you to do this adventure in spring or autumn, both seasons offer the best of the territory and, on warm days, some swimming opportunities.
region_intro: Gravel biking around Quesa, located in the interior of Valencia, Spain, offers diverse terrain for outdoor enthusiasts. The region features fertile agricultural lands, deep canyons carved by rivers like the Río Grande, and extensive pine forests. An extensive network of paths provides varied surfaces, from dirt tracks to unpaved segments, suitable for gravel cycling. The landscape includes significant elevation changes, with routes traversing limestone walls and leading to notable peaks. Best gravel bike trails around Quesa The most popular gravel bike trail is Circular gravel from Quesa to Caroche Peak, a 54.0 miles (87 km) trail that takes 7 hours 9 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through varied landscapes. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Gravel ride to Caroche peak and the Júcar river, a difficult 83.6 miles (135 km) path. This route traverses challenging terrain with extensive climbs and descents, exploring the Júcar river area. Local gravel bikers also love the Mortirolo of Quesa – El Caroche Summit loop from Quesa, a 65.2 miles (105 km) trail leading through mountainous areas with demanding ascents, often completed in about 9 hours 43 minutes. Gravel biking around Quesa is defined by deep canyons, lush pine forests, and significant elevation changes. The network offers options for experienced riders seeking challenging routes. The routes in Quesa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 20 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Quesa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on gravel bike trails around Quesa?
A: Gravel biking around Quesa offers a diverse landscape, ranging from fertile agricultural lands to deep canyons carved by rivers like the Río Grande, and extensive pine forests. You'll encounter varied surfaces, including dirt tracks and unpaved segments, with significant elevation changes as routes traverse limestone walls and lead to notable peaks. The region's extensive network of paths provides engaging challenges and diverse scenery.

Q: Are there any easy gravel bike trails in Quesa suitable for beginners or families?
A: The gravel bike trails currently listed for Quesa are generally considered difficult, featuring significant elevation gains and challenging terrain. For example, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31497718" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Millares – Casa del Empalme loop from Millares&lt;/a&gt; are rated as difficult. While the region offers an extensive network of paths, those specifically curated for gravel biking tend to cater to more experienced riders. It's advisable to check individual route details for specific difficulty levels and consider your experience before setting out.

Q: What are some of the most scenic natural attractions or viewpoints I can visit while gravel biking in Quesa?
A: Quesa is rich in natural beauty. You can explore the famous Los Charcos de Quesa, a series of natural pools with a spectacular waterfall at Charco del Chorro de Corbera. For panoramic views, consider visiting the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6282534" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tres Mollons Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or the Chapel of the Cross vantage point. The region also features deep canyons, lush pine forests, and the Voro Rock Shelter, known for its Levantine Cave Art.

Q: Can I find any trails that lead to waterfalls or natural pools in Quesa?
A: Yes, Quesa is famous for its natural pools and waterfalls. The most prominent are Los Charcos de Quesa, a series of four natural pools carved by the Río Grande, including Charco del Chorro de Corbera, which features a spectacular waterfall. While specific gravel routes directly to these spots aren't always listed, the extensive network of paths in the area means you can often combine cycling with a visit to these beautiful natural attractions.

Q: What is the best time of year to go gravel biking in Quesa?
A: Quesa, located in the interior of Valencia, Spain, generally experiences a Mediterranean climate. Spring (March to May) and Autumn (September to November) are typically ideal for gravel biking, offering pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather than the hot summer months or potentially cooler, wetter winter. Always check the local weather forecast before planning your ride.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites accessible by gravel bike in Quesa?
A: Absolutely. Quesa offers a blend of natural beauty and historical significance. You can explore the Moorish rammed earth remains of Quesa Castle (11th century) and the ancient irrigation channels and terraces of La Huerta del Lugar. The Voro Rock Shelter, a World Heritage site featuring Levantine Cave Art, is also accessible via the region's extensive network of paths, offering a unique cultural stop during your ride.

Q: What do other gravel bikers say about the trails in Quesa?
A: The gravel biking routes in Quesa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 20 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Quesa's varied terrain, often praising the challenging routes through deep canyons, lush pine forests, and the significant elevation changes that define the area.

Q: Are there any long-distance gravel routes for experienced riders in Quesa?
A: Yes, Quesa offers several challenging long-distance routes for experienced gravel bikers. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1668133715/gravel-ride-to-caroche-peak-and-the-jucar-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel ride to Caroche peak and the Júcar river&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 83.6 miles (135 km) path with extensive climbs and descents. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31497894" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mortirolo of Quesa – El Caroche Summit loop from Quesa&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 65.2 miles (105 km) through mountainous areas.

Q: Where can I find parking for gravel bike trails in Quesa?
A: While specific parking locations for each trail are not detailed in the route descriptions, Quesa is a small town with various access points to its extensive network of paths. It's generally advisable to look for public parking areas within or on the outskirts of Quesa town, or near known recreational areas like Los Charcos de Quesa, which are equipped with facilities. Always check local signage for parking regulations.

Q: Are there any specific peaks or summits I can aim for on a gravel bike ride in Quesa?
A: Yes, the Quesa region features several notable peaks that can be part of your gravel biking adventure. Routes often traverse towards areas like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6276620" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de Caroche&lt;/a&gt;. Other summits in the vicinity include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6278843" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Palm Grove&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2852802" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alt del Pollet (560 m)&lt;/a&gt;, offering challenging climbs and rewarding views.

Q: Are there any caves or interesting rock formations to explore near the gravel routes?
A: The Quesa area is home to several fascinating caves and rock formations. You can find highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4600612" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calicanto raft shelter&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4069862" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Araña Cave and La Rebolla Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4340471" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva del Tornero&lt;/a&gt;. These natural features add an extra layer of exploration to your gravel biking experience, often accessible via nearby trails.

Q: What kind of wildlife or protected areas might I encounter while cycling in Quesa?
A: Quesa is part of the European Natura 2000 network, designated as a Special Protection Area for Birds (ZEPA) and a Site of Community Importance (LIC). This means the area is rich in biodiversity. While cycling through the pine forests and canyons, you might spot various bird species and other Mediterranean wildlife. The commitment to conserving natural habitats makes for a rich natural experience.
tips: This section is very steep, if it is done in the exit direction from Quesa. However it offers you with spectacular views.
It is a spectacular stretch that runs parallel to the Fraile River. Throughout its journey you will pass through beautiful pools of transparent emerald waters.

The last kilometers of the ascent to the Caroig peak on the south face of the mountain are very technical due to the loose stone on the path. However, the scenery you go through and the views you get are second to none.
This group of houses is part of an old settlement located in a spectacular environment.
From the viewpoint there are beautiful views of the Cofrentes-Ayora Valley on the Pedrizas road towards the Alto del Cinto de Cabra.

An incredible site for its views and the arrival road to it. Going up Teresa de Cofrentes or Jalance, the route is amazing, with stretches of demanding slope but the views are more than rewarding. It is a round trip road so on the way back you will have to have your brakes ready ;)

Quesa is a medium-sized town in the interior of the province of Valencia belonging to the region of La Canal de Navarrés.

Despite its marked agricultural accent, Quesa also has a gypsum extraction industry. The action of the water on the soft ground of the area has created a landscape of ravines and pools ideal for bathing in the surroundings of the town.

waypoints.name: Alto del Cinto de la Cabra Viewpoint
